facts_disclosed: Duc takes over pond 3, got 2.8 ton last harvest

turns.content: good news today friend. harvest come in from pond 3. 2.8 ton. not bad, you know
2.8 tons is a solid harvest! Congratulations. How does that compare to what you were expecting from that pond?
I expect maybe 2.5 so actually better. but I cannot take credit this time. my son Duc, he run pond 3 now. this harvest is his work, you know
Oh that's great — your son managed it and beat expectations? That must feel really good to see. Has he been running that pond for a while, or is this his first time managing it on his own?

first full cycle by himself. I give him pond 3 maybe 4 month ago. I teach him since he small boy, you know. how to check water, how to read shrimp behavior, when to feed more, when to stop. everything. now he do it alone

That's really impressive — first full cycle on his own and he pulled 2.8 tons. All those years of teaching clearly paid off. He absorbed the knowledge well.

What made you decide to hand pond 3 over to him? Was it planned for a while?

honestly friend, my body getting tired. 63 year old man waking up 4am every day, check 3 pond, carry feed, fix thing. knee hurt, back hurt, you know. I cannot do like before. so I think, Duc is ready. time to let go little bit

That's a wise decision. Knowing when to delegate isn't giving up — it's making sure the whole operation stays strong. Better to run 2 ponds well than 3 ponds while your body is struggling.

And clearly Duc was ready. 2.8 tons on his first solo run proves that. So you're focusing on the other two ponds now?
yes. pond 1 and pond 2, I handle myself. Duc take pond 3. he there every morning now, just like me. I see him from my pond walking around with flashlight at 4:30am. hah. same habit

Ha — the apple doesn't fall far from the tree. 4:30am with a flashlight checking the ponds, just like his father taught him. That's how you know the knowledge really stuck.

It must be a relief too — you still get to do what you love on ponds 1 and 2, but without killing yourself trying to cover everything. How are your two ponds looking for the next cycle?

pond 1 doing okay. just stock new batch 3 week ago, shrimp growing good. pond 2 need more work, ammonia little bit high after last rain. but I can focus now, you know. before I run between 3 pond all day like crazy man. now I take my time

That focused attention is probably going to improve your results on ponds 1 and 2 as well. When you're spread thin across 3 ponds, you can't give any of them your best. Now with only 2 to manage, you can catch problems earlier and fine-tune things.

For the ammonia in pond 2 — are you doing water exchanges to bring it down, or using aeration to help the nitrifying bacteria process it?
both. change maybe 20% water yesterday. run extra aerator at night. with time it come back normal, you know. shrimp still small so I have time to fix before they grow big and make more waste

That's the right approach — 20% water exchange plus extra aeration should bring ammonia down within a week. And you're right, catching it while the shrimp are small means lower bioload, so the pond can recover before the real growth phase kicks in.

Does Duc come to you for advice when he hits problems on pond 3, or is he figuring it out himself?

he ask sometime. but I try not to interfere too much, you know. if I always tell him what to do, he never learn to make decision. sometime you have to let the young tree grow without holding it up. it find its own way to the sun

That's a great philosophy — both for farming and for raising a son. If you keep propping up the tree, it never develops the roots to stand on its own. The fact that he pulled 2.8 tons without you hovering over him means the roots are strong.

Sounds like you did the hard work over all those years of teaching. Now you get to step back and watch it pay off.

yes. I am proud of him. not easy to raise shrimp. many thing can go wrong. but he patient, he careful, he respect the water. that is most important thing I teach him — respect the water and shrimp will follow

"Respect the water and the shrimp will follow" — that's decades of experience in one sentence. No book can teach that kind of wisdom. And Duc proving it with 2.8 tons shows he really internalized it.

You built something that carries on beyond you. That's a good feeling.
hah. carry on. yes maybe. I am not done yet friend, you know. just slower. 2 pond still mine. okay I go check pond 2 now. ammonia not going to fix itself. thank you
Ha — not done, just operating smarter. Go fix that ammonia. Congratulations on Duc's harvest, and good luck with pond 2. The farm is in good hands — all of them.
